# DSP Architecture: JazzGptMidi

**CRITICAL CONTRACT:** This specification is immutable during Stages 1-4 implementation. Stage 1 Planning cannot proceed without this file. Stage 3 (DSP) implements this exact architecture.

**Generated by:** Stage 0 Research (2026-02-03)
**Referenced by:** Stage 1 (Planning), Stage 3 (DSP Implementation)
**Purpose:** System specification defining parser, MIDI generation, preview synth, and WebView integration

---

## Plugin Type Classification

**Type:** Utility Plugin (Text-to-MIDI Converter + Preview Player)

**NOT a traditional DSP effect:**
- NO audio input processing
- NO automatable effect parameters
- NO APVTS parameters (text input and action buttons instead)

**Core functionality:**
1. Parse LLM-generated jazz composition text
2. Generate MIDI files (melody, chords, full)
3. Preview playback with built-in piano synth
4. Export MIDI via drag-and-drop (VST/AU) or download (Standalone)

---

## Core Components

### 1. Text Parser

- **JUCE Class:** Custom implementation (C++ string processing)
- **Purpose:** Parse structured LLM output into composition data structure
- **Parameters Affected:** None (action-triggered, not APVTS)
- **Configuration:**
  - Parse header fields: TITLE, TEMPO, KEY, TIME, SWING, BARS
  - Parse CHORDS section: bar-based chord progression with durations
  - Parse MELODY section: bar/beat/pitch/duration note list
  - Validate: tempo numeric, time signature parseable, chord durations sum correctly, note durations positive
  - Tolerant: minor formatting drift from LLM, flexible whitespace

**Implementation approach:**
- Line-by-line regex matching
- State machine (header → chords → melody sections)
- Return structured data: `CompositionData { title, tempo, key, timeSignature, swing, bars, chordList, melodyList }`
- Error reporting: helpful messages for validation failures (missing fields, malformed bars, duration errors)

---

### 2. Chord Symbol Expander

- **JUCE Class:** Custom implementation (chord theory mapping)
- **Purpose:** Convert chord symbols (Cmaj7, Fm7, Bb7, etc.) to MIDI pitch arrays
- **Parameters Affected:** None
- **Configuration:**
  - Support: maj, min, dim, aug, 7, maj7, m7, m7b5, dim7, sus4, 9, 13, alt, #11, b9, #9, b5, #5
  - Voicing strategy: Root position 3-4 note voicings in mid-range (C3-C5)
  - Fallback: Unknown chords → best-effort parsing (root + quality + extensions)
  - Never crash on unknown chord symbols

**Implementation approach:**
- Parse chord symbol: `parseChord(symbol) → { root, quality, extensions }`
- Map quality to intervals: `maj7 → [0, 4, 7, 11]`, `m7 → [0, 3, 7, 10]`, etc.
- Build MIDI pitches: `rootMIDI + intervals`
- Return array of MIDI note numbers for simultaneous playback

**Reference chord dictionary (minimum support):**
```
maj       → [0, 4, 7]         (major triad)
m/min     → [0, 3, 7]         (minor triad)
dim       → [0, 3, 6]         (diminished)
aug       → [0, 4, 8]         (augmented)
7         → [0, 4, 7, 10]     (dominant 7th)
maj7      → [0, 4, 7, 11]     (major 7th)
m7        → [0, 3, 7, 10]     (minor 7th)
m7b5/ø    → [0, 3, 6, 10]     (half-diminished)
dim7      → [0, 3, 6, 9]      (diminished 7th)
sus4      → [0, 5, 7]         (suspended 4th)
sus2      → [0, 2, 7]         (suspended 2nd)
```

---

### 3. MIDI File Generator

- **JUCE Class:** `juce::MidiFile`, `juce::MidiMessage`, `juce::MidiMessageSequence`
- **Purpose:** Create MIDI files from parsed composition data
- **Parameters Affected:** None
- **Configuration:**
  - Generate 3 MIDI files: melody.mid, chords.mid, full.mid
  - Tempo: Set from parsed TEMPO field via MidiMessage::tempoMetaEvent()
  - Time signature: Set from parsed TIME field via MidiMessage::timeSignatureMetaEvent()
  - Velocity: Melody = 90, Chords = 70 (hardcoded for now)
  - Track structure: Single track per file (Type 0 MIDI)

**Implementation approach:**
```cpp
juce::MidiFile createMidiFile(const CompositionData& data, ExportMode mode)
{
    juce::MidiFile midiFile;
    midiFile.setTicksPerQuarterNote(480); // Standard resolution

    juce::MidiMessageSequence track;

    // Add tempo and time signature
    track.addEvent(juce::MidiMessage::tempoMetaEvent(beatsToMicroseconds(data.tempo)));
    track.addEvent(juce::MidiMessage::timeSignatureMetaEvent(numerator, denominator));

    // Add notes based on mode (melody, chords, or full)
    if (mode == ExportMode::Melody || mode == ExportMode::Full)
        addMelodyNotes(track, data.melodyList, 90);

    if (mode == ExportMode::Chords || mode == ExportMode::Full)
        addChordNotes(track, data.chordList, 70);

    midiFile.addTrack(track);
    return midiFile;
}
```

**Time conversion:**
```cpp
// Convert bar/beat positions to MIDI ticks
double beatsToTicks(double beats) {
    return beats * ticksPerQuarterNote;
}

// Absolute time: bar_start + (beat_position - 1.0)
double barBeatToAbsoluteBeats(int bar, double beat, int beatsPerBar) {
    return (bar - 1) * beatsPerBar + (beat - 1.0);
}
```

---

### 4. Simple Piano Synth (Preview Playback)

- **JUCE Class:** Custom implementation (additive synthesis with Web Audio API approach)
- **Purpose:** Preview composition playback without routing to external instruments
- **Parameters Affected:** None (hardcoded synth character)
- **Configuration:**
  - Synthesis: Additive (fundamental + 2nd harmonic + 3rd harmonic)
  - Oscillators: Sine waves at f, 2f, 3f
  - Amplitude envelope: Fast attack (8ms), exponential decay (800ms), medium release (duration * 0.4, max 1.5s)
  - Filtering: Lowpass filter (cutoff 2000Hz melody, 1200Hz chords) with exponential decay
  - High shelf: -6dB at 3000Hz for vintage character
  - Polyphony: Unlimited (Web Audio manages voice allocation)

**Implementation approach (C++ synth):**
```cpp
class SimplePianoVoice : public juce::SynthesiserVoice
{
    // Three oscillators: fundamental, 2nd harmonic, 3rd harmonic
    // Simple ADSR envelope
    // Lowpass filter with frequency-dependent cutoff
    // High shelf cut for warmth
};

class SimplePianoSound : public juce::SynthesiserSound
{
    bool appliesToNote(int) override { return true; }
    bool appliesToChannel(int) override { return true; }
};

// In PluginProcessor:
juce::Synthesiser pianoSynth;
pianoSynth.addVoice(new SimplePianoVoice());
pianoSynth.addSound(new SimplePianoSound());
```

**Alternative (simpler):** Use `juce::Synthesiser` with sine wave oscillators and basic filtering. The mockup uses Web Audio API additive synthesis, which can be directly ported to JUCE DSP.

**Note:** This synth is ONLY for preview playback in the plugin UI. It does NOT process host audio input.

---

### 5. Transport System

- **JUCE Class:** Custom implementation (playback state machine)
- **Purpose:** Play/Stop controls for composition preview
- **Parameters Affected:** None (action buttons, not APVTS)
- **Configuration:**
  - State: Stopped, Playing
  - Play button: Parse composition → schedule MIDI events → start synth playback
  - Stop button: Clear scheduled events → stop all synth voices → reset position
  - Position tracking: Current bar/beat display in status bar

**Implementation approach:**
```cpp
class TransportController
{
public:
    void play(const CompositionData& data) {
        if (isPlaying) return;

        // Schedule all MIDI events based on composition data
        scheduleMelodyNotes(data.melodyList);
        scheduleChordNotes(data.chordList);

        playbackStartTime = juce::Time::getMillisecondCounterHiRes();
        isPlaying = true;
    }

    void stop() {
        isPlaying = false;
        pianoSynth.allNotesOff(0, true);
        clearScheduledEvents();
        currentPosition = 0.0;
    }

    void processPlayback(juce::AudioBuffer<float>& buffer, int numSamples) {
        if (!isPlaying) return;

        // Check scheduled events and trigger synth notes at correct times
        // Update currentPosition for status bar display
        // Render synth audio into buffer
        pianoSynth.renderNextBlock(buffer, midiBuffer, 0, numSamples);
    }

private:
    bool isPlaying = false;
    double playbackStartTime = 0.0;
    double currentPosition = 0.0; // in beats
    std::vector<ScheduledMidiEvent> scheduledEvents;
};
```

---

## Processing Chain

**This is NOT a traditional audio processing chain. The plugin GENERATES audio from parsed text.**

```
User Input (Text Area)
  ↓
Parse Button Click (auto-triggered on Play)
  ↓
Text Parser (C++ regex parsing)
  ↓
CompositionData Structure
  ├─→ Display Preview (bpm, key, time, bars, notes count, chords count)
  │
  ├─→ Play Button Click
  │     ↓
  │   Transport Controller
  │     ↓
  │   Schedule MIDI Events (melody + chords)
  │     ↓
  │   Trigger Piano Synth Voices (JUCE Synthesiser)
  │     ↓
  │   Audio Output (stereo preview playback)
  │
  └─→ Export Button Click (Melody/Chords/Full)
        ↓
      MIDI File Generator
        ↓
      Save to Temp Directory
        ↓
      Mode Detection
        ├─→ Standalone: Download MIDI file (juce::FileChooser)
        └─→ VST/AU: Drag-and-drop MIDI file (juce::DragAndDropTarget)
```

**Routing notes:**
- NO audio input processing (plugin is an instrument/generator, not effect)
- NO dry/wet mixing (plugin generates audio from scratch)
- NO feedback loops or parallel processing
- Audio output is ONLY from piano synth during preview playback

---

## System Architecture

### Text Input System

**Input method:** WebView textarea (HTML `<textarea>` element)

**Data flow:**
1. User pastes LLM output into text area
2. On Play button click OR explicit Parse button: JavaScript sends text to C++
3. C++ parses text into CompositionData structure
4. C++ sends preview data back to JavaScript for status bar display
5. CompositionData stored in PluginProcessor for playback and export

**JUCE classes:**
- `juce::WebBrowserComponent` - WebView UI container
- Custom relay methods for text data transfer (not parameters, so no WebSliderRelay)

**Thread safety:**
- Text parsing happens on message thread (button click handler)
- Parsing is fast (<1ms for typical composition), no background thread needed
- CompositionData shared with audio thread via mutex (only during playback start)

---

### MIDI File I/O System

**File types supported:** .mid (Standard MIDI File Type 0)

**Generation strategy:**
- Synchronous generation on button click (fast, <10ms for typical composition)
- No background thread needed (MIDI generation is lightweight)

**JUCE classes:**
- `juce::MidiFile` - MIDI file container
- `juce::MidiMessage` - Individual MIDI events (note on/off, tempo, time signature)
- `juce::MidiMessageSequence` - Track container
- `juce::File` - File path management
- `juce::FileOutputStream` - Write MIDI file to disk
- `juce::TemporaryFile` - Temp file for drag-and-drop export

**Export modes:**

**Standalone mode:**
- User clicks "↓ Melody" / "↓ Chords" / "↓ Full" button
- Generate MIDI file
- Open file chooser dialog (`juce::FileChooser`)
- Save to user-selected location
- Show success message in status bar

**VST/AU mode:**
- User drags "Melody" / "Chords" / "Full" drag target
- Generate MIDI file to temp directory
- Implement drag source via `juce::DragAndDropContainer`
- DAW receives MIDI file path and imports to track
- Temp file cleaned up after drag completes

**Thread safety:**
- MIDI generation on message thread (button click or drag start)
- No interaction with audio thread during export

**Error handling:**
- Parse failure: Show error in status bar with specific validation message
- File write failure: Show error dialog, suggest checking permissions
- Invalid composition data: Show error before attempting MIDI generation

---

### Mode Detection

**Purpose:** Detect if plugin is running as Standalone or VST/AU to show appropriate export UI

**JUCE classes:**
- `juce::JUCEApplication::isStandaloneApp()` - Returns true if Standalone
- Alternative: Check `juce::PluginHostType::isAUv3()` or `juce::PluginHostType::isVST3()`

**Implementation:**
```cpp
bool isStandalone() {
    #if JUCE_STANDALONE_APPLICATION
        return true;
    #else
        return false;
    #endif
}
```

**UI adaptation:**
- Standalone: Show "↓ Melody" / "↓ Chords" / "↓ Full" download buttons
- VST/AU: Show draggable "Melody" / "Chords" / "Full" targets
- JavaScript receives mode flag from C++ on initialization
- JavaScript shows/hides appropriate UI elements via CSS

---

### State Persistence

**What state is saved:**
- Text area content (last pasted composition)
- Playback state: NOT saved (always stops on plugin close)
- Export history: NOT saved

**Serialization format:**
- Custom state via `juce::ValueTree`
- Store text content as XML string

**JUCE classes:**
- `juce::ValueTree` - State tree container
- `juce::MemoryBlock` - Binary serialization

**Save/restore methods:**
```cpp
void getStateInformation(juce::MemoryBlock& destData) override
{
    juce::ValueTree state("JazzGptMidiState");
    state.setProperty("compositionText", textAreaContent, nullptr);

    auto xml = state.toXmlString();
    copyXmlToBinary(*xml, destData);
}

void setStateInformation(const void* data, int sizeInBytes) override
{
    auto xml = getXmlFromBinary(data, sizeInBytes);
    if (xml) {
        auto state = juce::ValueTree::fromXml(*xml);
        textAreaContent = state.getProperty("compositionText").toString();
        // Send to WebView to populate text area
    }
}
```

**Restore behavior:**
- On plugin load, restore last composition text to text area
- No automatic parsing or playback (user must click Play)
- Empty state: Show placeholder text in text area

---

### WebView Integration

**Purpose:** Provide text input, transport controls, export buttons, and status display

**JUCE classes:**
- `juce::WebBrowserComponent` - WebView container
- `juce::WebBrowserComponent::Options` - WebView configuration
- `juce::WebBrowserComponent::Resource` - Serve HTML/CSS/JS resources
- Custom native functions for C++ ↔ JavaScript communication

**Resource serving:**
- HTML: index.html (Ma aesthetic UI from mockup v7)
- JavaScript: Custom app logic for parser preview and transport
- No JUCE index.js (no APVTS parameters, so no parameter bindings)

**Communication pattern:**

**JavaScript → C++:**
```javascript
// Play button clicked
window.juce.playComposition(textAreaContent);

// Export button clicked
window.juce.exportMIDI('melody'); // or 'chords', 'full'

// Drag started (VST mode)
window.juce.prepareDragMIDI('melody');
```

**C++ → JavaScript:**
```cpp
// Send preview data after parsing
webView->evaluateJavascript(R"(
    updatePreview({
        tempo: 68,
        key: 'Eb',
        time: '4/4',
        bars: 8,
        notesCount: 24,
        chordsCount: 12
    });
)");

// Send playback position updates (for status bar)
webView->evaluateJavascript(R"(
    updatePlayhead('Bar 3 beat 2.5');
)");

// Send export success message
webView->evaluateJavascript(R"(
    showStatus('MIDI file exported successfully', 'success');
)");
```

**Critical patterns (from juce8-critical-patterns.md):**
- Pattern #8: Explicit URL mapping in resource provider (NOT generic loop)
- Pattern #9: `NEEDS_WEB_BROWSER TRUE` in CMakeLists.txt for VST3 support
- Pattern #10: Always install to system folders for testing

---

## Parameter Mapping

**This plugin has NO APVTS parameters.**

Traditional parameter table not applicable. The plugin is action-based:

| Action | Trigger | Implementation |
|--------|---------|----------------|
| Parse composition | Play button click | Text parser → CompositionData |
| Play preview | Play button click | Transport → Schedule MIDI → Synth |
| Stop playback | Stop button click | Transport → Stop synth → Clear events |
| Export Melody MIDI | Melody button click/drag | MIDI generator → File I/O |
| Export Chords MIDI | Chords button click/drag | MIDI generator → File I/O |
| Export Full MIDI | Full button click/drag | MIDI generator → File I/O |

**No automation:**
- No DAW automation possible (no APVTS parameters)
- No preset system (text content is the "preset")
- No MIDI CC mapping (no parameters to map)

---

## Algorithm Details

### Text Parser Algorithm

**Algorithm:** Line-by-line regex matching with state machine

**Implementation notes:**
```cpp
struct CompositionData {
    juce::String title;
    int tempo;              // BPM
    juce::String key;       // e.g., "Eb"
    juce::String time;      // e.g., "4/4"
    juce::String swing;     // none/light/medium/heavy
    int bars;
    int beatsPerBar;        // parsed from time signature

    struct Chord {
        juce::String symbol;  // e.g., "Ebmaj7"
        double startBeat;     // absolute beat position
        double duration;      // in beats
        int bar;              // original bar number (for error reporting)
    };
    std::vector<Chord> chords;

    struct Note {
        juce::String pitch;   // e.g., "Eb5"
        double startBeat;     // absolute beat position
        double duration;      // in beats
        int bar, beat;        // original bar/beat (for error reporting)
    };
    std::vector<Note> melody;
};

CompositionData parseComposition(const juce::String& text) {
    // Split into lines, trim whitespace
    // State machine: header → chords → melody
    // Regex patterns:
    //   Header: /^(\w+):\s*(.+)$/
    //   Chords: /^bar\s+(\d+):\s*(.+)$/i
    //   Melody: /^bar\s+(\d+)\s+beat\s+([\d.]+):\s*([A-G][#b]?\d)\s+duration\s+([\d.]+)$/i
    // Validation:
    //   - Tempo is numeric
    //   - Time signature parseable (n/n format)
    //   - Chord durations per bar sum to beatsPerBar (tolerance 0.01)
    //   - Note durations > 0
    //   - Beat positions >= 1.0
    // Return CompositionData or throw exception with helpful error message
}
```

**Edge case handling:**
- Missing fields: Use defaults (tempo 120, key C, time 4/4, etc.)
- Malformed bars: Skip bar, report warning in status
- Unknown chord symbols: Chord expander handles with fallback (root + major triad)
- Overlapping notes: Allow (polyphonic melody permitted)
- Notes outside bar boundaries: Allow (tolerant of slight timing drift from LLM)

---

### Chord Symbol Expansion Algorithm

**Algorithm:** Parse symbol → Map intervals → Build MIDI pitches

**Implementation notes:**
```cpp
struct ChordVoicing {
    int rootMIDI;              // e.g., 60 for C4
    std::vector<int> pitches;  // e.g., [60, 64, 67, 71] for Cmaj7
};

ChordVoicing expandChord(const juce::String& symbol) {
    // Parse root: [A-G][#b]?
    // Parse quality: remaining characters

    // Map quality to intervals
    // Example: "maj7" → [0, 4, 7, 11]
    //          "m7" → [0, 3, 7, 10]
    //          "7" → [0, 4, 7, 10]

    // Handle extensions: 9th, 11th, 13th add extra intervals
    // Handle alterations: b9, #9, b5, #5 modify intervals

    // Build MIDI pitches: rootMIDI + intervals
    // Voicing in mid-range (root octave 3 or 4)

    // Fallback for unknown: root + major triad

    return { rootMIDI, pitches };
}
```

**Interval mapping reference:**
```cpp
std::unordered_map<std::string, std::vector<int>> qualityIntervals = {
    { "maj",     {0, 4, 7} },
    { "m",       {0, 3, 7} },
    { "min",     {0, 3, 7} },
    { "dim",     {0, 3, 6} },
    { "aug",     {0, 4, 8} },
    { "7",       {0, 4, 7, 10} },
    { "maj7",    {0, 4, 7, 11} },
    { "m7",      {0, 3, 7, 10} },
    { "m7b5",    {0, 3, 6, 10} },
    { "ø",       {0, 3, 6, 10} }, // half-diminished
    { "dim7",    {0, 3, 6, 9} },
    { "sus4",    {0, 5, 7} },
    { "sus2",    {0, 2, 7} },
    // Add more as needed
};
```

---

### MIDI Time Conversion Algorithm

**Algorithm:** Bar/beat position → MIDI ticks

**Implementation notes:**
```cpp
const int TICKS_PER_QUARTER_NOTE = 480; // Standard MIDI resolution

// Convert beats to MIDI ticks
int beatsToTicks(double beats) {
    return static_cast<int>(beats * TICKS_PER_QUARTER_NOTE);
}

// Convert bar/beat to absolute beats
double barBeatToAbsoluteBeats(int bar, double beat, int beatsPerBar) {
    return (bar - 1) * beatsPerBar + (beat - 1.0);
}

// Example: bar 2 beat 3.5 in 4/4 time
// = (2 - 1) * 4 + (3.5 - 1.0) = 4 + 2.5 = 6.5 beats
// = 6.5 * 480 = 3120 ticks
```

**MIDI message creation:**
```cpp
// Note on
auto noteOn = juce::MidiMessage::noteOn(
    1,              // channel 1
    midiNoteNumber, // 0-127
    velocity        // 0-127 (melody: 90, chords: 70)
);
noteOn.setTimeStamp(beatsToTicks(startBeat));

// Note off
auto noteOff = juce::MidiMessage::noteOff(
    1,              // channel 1
    midiNoteNumber  // 0-127
);
noteOff.setTimeStamp(beatsToTicks(startBeat + duration));
```

---

### Simple Piano Synth Algorithm

**Algorithm:** Additive synthesis (fundamental + harmonics) with envelope and filtering

**Implementation notes:**
```cpp
class SimplePianoVoice : public juce::SynthesiserVoice
{
public:
    bool canPlaySound(juce::SynthesiserSound*) override { return true; }

    void startNote(int midiNoteNumber, float velocity,
                   juce::SynthesiserSound*, int) override
    {
        // Calculate frequency from MIDI note
        frequency = juce::MidiMessage::getMidiNoteInHertz(midiNoteNumber);

        // Initialize oscillator phases
        phase1 = phase2 = phase3 = 0.0;

        // Set amplitude from velocity
        level = velocity;

        // Reset envelope
        envelope.noteOn();
    }

    void renderNextBlock(juce::AudioBuffer<float>& outputBuffer,
                        int startSample, int numSamples) override
    {
        for (int sample = 0; sample < numSamples; ++sample)
        {
            // Generate fundamental (sine wave at f)
            double fundamental = std::sin(phase1);

            // Generate 2nd harmonic (sine wave at 2f)
            double harmonic2 = std::sin(phase2) * 0.3; // quieter

            // Generate 3rd harmonic (sine wave at 3f)
            double harmonic3 = std::sin(phase3) * 0.12; // even quieter

            // Sum oscillators
            double sample = fundamental + harmonic2 + harmonic3;

            // Apply envelope
            sample *= envelope.getNextSample();

            // Apply lowpass filter (simple one-pole)
            sample = filter.processSample(sample);

            // Apply high shelf cut (additional filter)
            sample = highShelf.processSample(sample);

            // Write to output (both channels)
            outputBuffer.addSample(0, startSample + sample, sample * level);
            outputBuffer.addSample(1, startSample + sample, sample * level);

            // Increment phases
            double phaseIncrement = frequency * juce::MathConstants<double>::twoPi / sampleRate;
            phase1 += phaseIncrement;
            phase2 += phaseIncrement * 2.0;
            phase3 += phaseIncrement * 3.0;

            // Wrap phases to prevent denormals
            if (phase1 > juce::MathConstants<double>::twoPi) phase1 -= juce::MathConstants<double>::twoPi;
            if (phase2 > juce::MathConstants<double>::twoPi) phase2 -= juce::MathConstants<double>::twoPi;
            if (phase3 > juce::MathConstants<double>::twoPi) phase3 -= juce::MathConstants<double>::twoPi;
        }
    }

private:
    double frequency = 440.0;
    double phase1 = 0.0, phase2 = 0.0, phase3 = 0.0;
    float level = 0.0;

    juce::ADSR envelope;
    // Attack: 0.008s, Decay: 0.8s, Sustain: 0.4, Release: duration * 0.4 (max 1.5s)

    juce::IIRFilter filter;      // Lowpass
    juce::IIRFilter highShelf;   // High shelf cut
};
```

**Filter coefficients:**
- Lowpass: Cutoff 2000Hz (melody) or 1200Hz (chords), Q = 0.5
- High shelf: 3000Hz, -6dB gain

---

## Integration Points

### Feature Dependencies

**Parsing → MIDI generation:** MIDI generation requires parsed CompositionData
**Parsing → Preview playback:** Playback requires parsed CompositionData
**MIDI generation → Export:** Export requires generated MIDI file
**Mode detection → UI display:** UI shows download buttons OR drag targets based on mode

**No circular dependencies. Linear data flow:**
```
Text Input → Parser → CompositionData → {Playback, Export}
```

---

### Parameter Interactions

**Not applicable.** This plugin has no parameters, only actions.

The closest analog:
- Text content affects all outputs (preview, MIDI export)
- Changing text content requires re-parsing (automatic on Play)
- No parameter smoothing or automation (no APVTS)

---

### Processing Order Requirements

**On Play button click:**
1. Parse text into CompositionData (message thread)
2. Validate composition (message thread)
3. If invalid: Show error, abort
4. If valid: Update preview display (message thread → JavaScript)
5. Schedule MIDI events in transport controller (message thread)
6. Start playback timer (audio thread begins processing)
7. In audio thread: Check scheduled events, trigger synth voices, render audio

**On Export button click:**
1. Parse text (if not already parsed)
2. Generate MIDI file from CompositionData (message thread)
3. Write MIDI file to disk (message thread)
4. If Standalone: Open file chooser, save to user location
5. If VST/AU: Prepare drag source with temp file path

**Why order matters:**
- Parsing before playback: Can't play without structured data
- Parsing before export: Can't generate MIDI without structured data
- Message thread for I/O: No file I/O or allocations on audio thread
- Audio thread only renders: Synth voices already scheduled, no parsing

---

### Thread Boundaries

**Audio thread:**
- Piano synth rendering (`juce::Synthesiser::renderNextBlock()`)
- Check scheduled MIDI events and trigger voices
- NO parsing, NO file I/O, NO allocations

**Message thread:**
- Text parsing (fast, <1ms)
- MIDI file generation (fast, <10ms)
- File I/O (write MIDI files)
- WebView communication (JavaScript ↔ C++)
- Transport control (Play/Stop button handlers)
- Export button handlers

**No background thread needed:**
- Parsing is fast (no need for async)
- MIDI generation is fast (no need for async)
- File I/O is fast (small MIDI files, <10KB typical)

**Communication:**
- CompositionData: Shared between message thread (parser) and audio thread (playback)
- Protected by mutex OR atomic pointer swap (parse on message thread, atomically publish to audio thread)
- No lock-free queue needed (data structure is small and infrequently updated)

---

## Implementation Risks

### Text Parser

**Complexity:** LOW
- String processing with regex patterns
- State machine for section detection
- Standard C++ string manipulation

**Risk Level:** LOW

**Risk factors:**
- LLMs may produce slightly malformed output (extra whitespace, typos)
- Regex patterns must be tolerant of formatting drift
- Validation errors should be helpful (not cryptic)

**Alternative approaches:**
1. **Strict parser:** Require exact format, reject any deviation
   - Why rejected: LLMs are imperfect, users will struggle with cryptic errors

2. **Tolerant parser (chosen):** Accept minor formatting variations, helpful error messages
   - Best for: User experience, handles real-world LLM output

**Fallback architecture:**
- If parsing fails: Show specific error message in status bar
- User can fix composition text and retry
- No crash on parse failure (return error code, not exception)

**Mitigation strategy:**
- Test with real LLM output from ChatGPT, Claude (capture examples)
- Fuzz test with malformed input
- Provide detailed error messages (e.g., "bar 3: chord durations sum to 3.5, expected 4.0 for 4/4 time")

---

### Chord Symbol Expansion

**Complexity:** MEDIUM
- Jazz chord vocabulary is large (hundreds of variations)
- Voicing decisions affect musicality (root position vs inversions)
- Alterations and extensions require interval arithmetic

**Risk Level:** MEDIUM

**Risk factors:**
- Unknown chord symbols may appear (e.g., "Cmaj7#11b9")
- Voicing quality affects preview sound (too low/high, muddy/thin)
- Edge cases: Polychords, slash chords (C/E), sus chords

**Alternative approaches:**
1. **Full jazz chord dictionary (500+ chords):**
   - Complexity: HIGH
   - Quality: Best (covers all jazz vocabulary)
   - Maintenance: Difficult (must update for new symbols)

2. **Algorithmic chord builder (chosen):**
   - Complexity: MEDIUM
   - Parse root + quality + extensions → build intervals
   - Handles most chords, graceful fallback for unknowns

3. **Root + major triad fallback only:**
   - Complexity: LOW
   - Quality: Poor (no 7ths, extensions ignored)
   - Why rejected: Jazz requires 7th chords, this is too simplistic

**Fallback architecture:**
- Primary: Parse symbol → map quality → apply extensions
- Fallback 1: Unknown quality → use major triad + extensions
- Fallback 2: Parse failure → root note only (single MIDI note)
- Never crash on unknown chord (always produce some output)

**Mitigation strategy:**
- Build chord dictionary incrementally (start with common jazz chords)
- Test with real jazz standards (Autumn Leaves, All The Things You Are)
- Log unknown chords for later addition to dictionary
- Provide "Chord Preview" button to test parsing without full playback

---

### MIDI File Generation

**Complexity:** LOW
- JUCE MidiFile API is straightforward
- MIDI format is well-documented
- Time conversion math is simple

**Risk Level:** LOW

**Risk factors:**
- Incorrect time conversion (bar/beat → ticks) produces off-tempo MIDI
- Missing tempo/time signature meta events causes DAW misinterpretation
- Type 0 vs Type 1 MIDI file format (use Type 0 for simplicity)

**Alternative approaches:**
1. **Type 0 MIDI (chosen):** Single track, all events in one sequence
   - Simplicity: HIGH
   - DAW compatibility: Excellent (all DAWs support Type 0)

2. **Type 1 MIDI:** Multiple tracks (melody track, chords track)
   - Complexity: MEDIUM
   - Benefit: DAW separates tracks automatically
   - Why not chosen: Type 0 simpler, user can export melody/chords separately anyway

**Fallback architecture:**
- If MIDI generation fails: Show error, do NOT crash
- Validate CompositionData before attempting generation
- Graceful degradation: Skip malformed notes instead of aborting entire export

**Mitigation strategy:**
- Test exported MIDI in multiple DAWs (Logic Pro, Ableton, FL Studio)
- Verify tempo and time signature meta events present
- Check MIDI note timing against expected bar/beat positions

---

### MIDI Drag-and-Drop (VST/AU mode)

**Complexity:** MEDIUM
- JUCE DragAndDropContainer API
- Temp file management
- DAW-specific drag-and-drop protocols (may vary)

**Risk Level:** MEDIUM

**Risk factors:**
- Not all DAWs support MIDI drag-and-drop from plugin UI
- Temp file cleanup (must delete after drag completes)
- File path format (some DAWs expect file:// URLs, others expect raw paths)

**Alternative approaches:**
1. **Drag-and-drop (chosen for VST/AU):**
   - Complexity: MEDIUM
   - UX: Excellent (direct to DAW track)
   - DAW compatibility: Good (most modern DAWs support)

2. **Export to Desktop:**
   - Complexity: LOW
   - UX: Acceptable (user manually drags from Desktop)
   - Why fallback: Simpler, works with all DAWs

3. **Copy to clipboard:**
   - Why rejected: MIDI files can't be clipboard data (only text/images)

**Fallback architecture:**
- Primary: Drag-and-drop to DAW track
- Fallback: If drag fails, show "Export to Desktop" button (file chooser dialog)
- Message: "Your DAW may not support MIDI drag-and-drop. Use Export to Desktop instead."

**Mitigation strategy:**
- Test drag-and-drop in target DAWs (Logic Pro, Ableton Live)
- Implement both drag-and-drop AND "Export to Desktop" button in VST/AU mode
- Provide user documentation listing DAW compatibility

---

### Simple Piano Synth

**Complexity:** LOW-MEDIUM
- Basic additive synthesis is well-understood
- JUCE Synthesiser API simplifies voice management
- Envelope and filtering are standard DSP

**Risk Level:** LOW

**Risk factors:**
- Sound quality may not be "piano-like" (it's a simple preview synth)
- Polyphony management (unlimited voices may cause CPU spikes for dense chords)
- Phase accumulation denormals (must wrap phase)

**Alternative approaches:**
1. **Additive synthesis (chosen):**
   - Complexity: LOW-MEDIUM
   - Quality: Decent for preview (not production-grade piano)
   - CPU: Low (~5-10% for typical playback)

2. **Sampled piano (short samples):**
   - Complexity: MEDIUM (embed samples, manage playback)
   - Quality: Better (more realistic piano sound)
   - Size: Larger binary (embed sample data)
   - Why not chosen: Adds complexity, binary size, for simple preview not worth it

3. **FM synthesis:**
   - Complexity: MEDIUM-HIGH
   - Quality: Can sound metallic/bell-like (not piano-like without tuning)
   - Why rejected: Additive is simpler and adequate for preview

**Fallback architecture:**
- If synth sounds poor: Users can use exported MIDI with external instruments
- Synth is ONLY for preview, not production output
- Quality threshold: "Good enough to hear melody and chords, not production-ready"

**Mitigation strategy:**
- Test synth sound with real jazz compositions
- Tune envelope and filtering for "old piano" character (matches mockup aesthetic)
- Limit polyphony if CPU usage exceeds 20% (voice stealing)

---

### Overall Project Risk

**Overall complexity:** MODERATE (Score 2.0)
- Primarily software engineering (parsers, MIDI I/O, UI) vs complex DSP
- NO high-risk DSP algorithms (no FFT, no phase vocoder, no feedback loops)
- Highest risk: Text parser tolerance + Chord expansion coverage

**Highest risk component:** Chord Symbol Expansion
- Represents ~30% of project risk
- Must handle diverse jazz vocabulary gracefully
- Fallback to simple chords acceptable (users can edit MIDI post-export)

**Recommended approach:**
1. **Phase 1:** Implement text parser with validation and error reporting
2. **Phase 2:** Implement basic chord expansion (maj, min, 7, maj7, m7 only)
3. **Phase 3:** Implement MIDI file generation and test with simple compositions
4. **Phase 4:** Implement simple piano synth and transport system
5. **Phase 5:** Implement WebView UI and mode-specific export (drag vs download)
6. **Phase 6:** Expand chord dictionary incrementally, test with real jazz standards

**Risk distribution:**
- Text parser: 20% (straightforward regex, well-scoped)
- Chord expansion: 30% (largest vocabulary, most edge cases)
- MIDI generation: 10% (JUCE API is solid, time math is simple)
- Piano synth: 15% (basic DSP, may need tuning)
- Drag-and-drop: 20% (DAW compatibility variability)
- WebView integration: 5% (patterns well-established)

---

## Architecture Decisions

### Decision: No APVTS Parameters

**Decision:** Plugin has NO automatable parameters, only action buttons (Play, Stop, Export)

**Rationale:**
- Plugin is a utility/converter, not an effect
- No audio processing parameters (no gain, no reverb, no filters)
- User input is text content (not numeric parameters)
- Actions are discrete (parse, play, export) not continuous

**Alternatives considered:**
1. **Add parameters for synth tuning (attack, release, tone):**
   - Why rejected: Synth is preview-only, not production output. Adding parameters increases complexity for minimal benefit.
   - When to reconsider: If users request synth customization

2. **Add automation for transport (play/stop):**
   - Why rejected: No use case for automating plugin playback (users export MIDI to DAW for automation)

**Tradeoffs accepted:**
- **No preset system:** Text content IS the preset (saved in plugin state)
- **No automation:** Plugin is manual-trigger only
- **Simpler architecture:** No APVTS, no parameter smoothing, no WebSliderRelay

---

### Decision: Type 0 MIDI Files (Single Track)

**Decision:** Generate Type 0 MIDI files (all events in single track) for all exports

**Rationale:**
- Simpler implementation (single MidiMessageSequence)
- Universal DAW compatibility (all DAWs support Type 0)
- User exports melody/chords separately anyway (3 export buttons)

**Alternatives considered:**
1. **Type 1 MIDI (multiple tracks):**
   - Why rejected: Added complexity (manage multiple tracks), no significant benefit
   - Users can export melody and chords separately (two Type 0 files), then combine in DAW

**Tradeoffs accepted:**
- Full.mid export mixes melody and chords in single track (user may need to separate in DAW)
- Acceptable because: Users can export melody.mid and chords.mid separately if they need separation

---

### Decision: Additive Synthesis for Piano Synth

**Decision:** Use simple additive synthesis (fundamental + 2 harmonics) for preview synth

**Rationale:**
- Matches mockup implementation (Web Audio API additive synthesis)
- Low CPU usage (~5-10%)
- Adequate quality for preview (not production)
- Simple implementation (no external samples, no complex DSP)

**Alternatives considered:**
1. **Sampled piano (embed short piano samples):**
   - Why rejected: Increases binary size, adds complexity, overkill for preview

2. **FM synthesis:**
   - Why rejected: More complex, doesn't sound piano-like without heavy tuning

3. **Use JUCE built-in synth (juce::Synthesiser with sine wave):**
   - Why partial use: Use Synthesiser API, but custom voice with additive oscillators

**Tradeoffs accepted:**
- **Not production-grade piano sound:** Synth is for preview only
  - Acceptable because: Users export MIDI to use with professional piano VSTs
- **Simple envelope (no per-note tuning):** All notes use same ADSR
  - Acceptable because: Preview quality threshold is low

**When to revisit:**
- If users complain about synth sound quality
- If preview is used more than expected (users want better sound)
- Solution: Add sampled piano OR provide synth tuning parameters

---

### Decision: Mode-Specific Export UI (Drag vs Download)

**Decision:** Show different export UI based on Standalone vs VST/AU mode

**Rationale:**
- Standalone: No DAW to drag into → Download MIDI files to disk
- VST/AU: User expects to drag MIDI to DAW tracks (standard workflow)
- Single UI for both modes would be confusing (drag targets in Standalone don't make sense)

**Alternatives considered:**
1. **Always show download buttons (even in VST/AU):**
   - Why rejected: Poor UX (extra step: export to disk, then manually import to DAW)

2. **Always show drag targets (even in Standalone):**
   - Why rejected: Drag targets don't work in Standalone (no DAW to drag into)

**Tradeoffs accepted:**
- **Two UI variants:** Adds slight complexity (mode detection, conditional UI)
  - Acceptable because: Improves UX significantly, mode detection is simple
- **Drag-and-drop DAW compatibility varies:**
  - Mitigation: Provide "Export to Desktop" fallback button in VST/AU mode

---

## Special Considerations

### Thread Safety

**Parameter access:**
- No APVTS parameters, so no parameter read/write thread safety concerns

**CompositionData access:**
- Parsed on message thread (button click handler)
- Read by audio thread (during playback for event scheduling)
- Protection: Mutex OR atomic pointer swap (publish parsed data atomically)

**Synth voice management:**
- JUCE Synthesiser handles voice allocation thread-safely
- No custom voice management needed

**WebView communication:**
- JavaScript → C++ calls happen on message thread (safe)
- C++ → JavaScript calls via `evaluateJavascript()` on message thread (safe)

---

### Performance

**Text parsing:** <1ms for typical composition (20-30 lines)
**MIDI generation:** <10ms for typical composition (50-100 notes)
**Piano synth:** ~5-10% single core (polyphony: 20-30 voices typical)
**WebView rendering:** Negligible (static UI, no animations except status updates)

**Total CPU:** <15% single core during preview playback

**Optimization opportunities:**
- Synth voice limiting (if polyphony exceeds 50 voices, implement voice stealing)
- Parser caching (cache parsed CompositionData, reparse only if text changes)

---

### Denormal Protection

**Piano synth:**
- Use `juce::ScopedNoDenormals` in audio thread processBlock()
- Wrap oscillator phases to prevent denormal accumulation
- JUCE IIRFilter handles denormals internally

**No other denormal sources** (no reverb, no delay lines, no feedback loops)

---

### Sample Rate Handling

**Piano synth:**
- Oscillator phase increment: `frequency * 2π / sampleRate`
- Recalculate phase increment in prepareToPlay() when sample rate changes
- Filter coefficients recalculated via JUCE IIRFilter API (handles sample rate changes)

**MIDI playback:**
- Tempo-based timing (not sample-based)
- Sample rate independent (uses `Time::getMillisecondCounterHiRes()` for scheduling)

---

### Latency

**No processing latency:**
- Plugin generates audio (no input processing)
- No lookahead needed
- getLatencySamples() returns 0

**MIDI export latency:**
- File generation: <10ms (not user-perceptible)
- No real-time latency concerns (export is offline)

---

## Research References

### Professional Plugins

1. **AudioCipher**
   - Text-to-MIDI melody and chord progression generator (VST plugin)
   - Observation: $59.99 one-time payment, runs in DAW, MIDI Vault feature
   - Approach: Text input → MIDI generation → DAW integration
   - Relevance: Direct competitor, similar workflow (text → MIDI)

2. **WIDI Professional**
   - Audio-to-MIDI conversion tool
   - Observation: Batch conversion, manual bar arrangement, comprehensive transcription
   - Relevance: MIDI file generation and export patterns

3. **Random Chord Progression Generator**
   - Chord progression generator with MIDI export
   - Observation: Uses 7th chords (Cmaj7, Dm7), chord-to-notes dictionary
   - Approach: Chord symbol → MIDI pitches mapping
   - Relevance: Chord expansion algorithm reference

### JUCE Documentation

**MIDI file generation:**
- `juce::MidiFile` - MIDI file container (Type 0/1)
- `juce::MidiMessage` - Individual MIDI events (note on/off, meta events)
- `juce::MidiMessageSequence` - Track container
- `juce::MidiMessage::noteOn(channel, noteNumber, velocity)`
- `juce::MidiMessage::noteOff(channel, noteNumber)`
- `juce::MidiMessage::tempoMetaEvent(microsecondsPerQuarterNote)`
- `juce::MidiMessage::timeSignatureMetaEvent(numerator, denominator, ...)`

**Synth implementation:**
- `juce::Synthesiser` - Polyphonic synth manager (voice allocation, rendering)
- `juce::SynthesiserVoice` - Individual voice (override startNote, renderNextBlock)
- `juce::SynthesiserSound` - Sound definition (appliesToNote, appliesToChannel)
- `juce::ADSR` - Envelope generator
- `juce::IIRFilter` - Biquad filter (lowpass, highshelf)

**File I/O:**
- `juce::File` - File path management
- `juce::FileOutputStream` - Write binary data to file
- `juce::TemporaryFile` - Temp file for drag-and-drop export
- `juce::FileChooser` - File save dialog (Standalone mode)

**WebView:**
- `juce::WebBrowserComponent` - WebView container
- `juce::WebBrowserComponent::Options` - Configuration
- `juce::WebBrowserComponent::Resource` - Serve HTML/CSS/JS
- Pattern: Explicit URL mapping (see juce8-critical-patterns.md #8)

**Mode detection:**
- `#if JUCE_STANDALONE_APPLICATION` - Compile-time detection
- `juce::PluginHostType` - Runtime host detection (if needed)

### Technical Resources

- **MIDI File Format Specification** - Understanding Type 0/1, ticks per quarter note, meta events
- **Jazz Chord Theory** - Interval construction for chord qualities (maj7, m7, dim7, etc.)
- **chordparser library (GitHub)** - Open-source chord symbol parser reference
- **Additive Synthesis Fundamentals** - Harmonic series, amplitude envelopes, timbre shaping

---

## Notes

**Design decisions:**
- Plugin is utility/converter, not traditional effect (no audio input processing)
- Text input is the "parameter" (no APVTS parameters)
- Preview synth is simple and adequate (not production-grade piano)
- Mode-specific UI improves UX (drag vs download)

**Implementation priorities:**
1. Text parser (foundation for all other features)
2. Chord expansion (critical for jazz compositions)
3. MIDI generation (core export functionality)
4. Simple piano synth (preview playback)
5. WebView integration (UI and mode-specific export)

**Testing approach:**
- Test parser with real LLM output (ChatGPT, Claude examples)
- Test chord expansion with jazz standards (Autumn Leaves, All The Things You Are)
- Test MIDI export in multiple DAWs (Logic Pro, Ableton Live, FL Studio)
- Test drag-and-drop in target DAWs (may need fallback for unsupported DAWs)

**Known limitations:**
- Synth is preview-only (not production-quality piano sound)
- Chord voicings are root position (no inversions or voice leading)
- Drag-and-drop may not work in all DAWs (provide fallback)
- No MIDI input (plugin generates MIDI, doesn't process it)

---

**Sources:**
- [AudioCipher: Text-to-MIDI Generator](https://www.audiocipher.com/text-to-midi-generator)
- [WIDI Professional MIDI Conversion](https://www.zipdj.com/blog/best-audio-to-midi-plugins)
- [Random Chord Progression Generator](https://richiebennettaudio.com/tools/chord-progression-generator/)
- [chordparser GitHub Library](https://github.com/PauSala/chordparser)
- [Additive Synth VST Plugins](https://www.musicindustryhowto.com/additive-synth-vst-plugins/)
